A Minimal Book Example Chapter 1 Prerequisites

A Minimal Book Example Yihui Xie 2021-07-06 Chapter 1 Prerequisites This is a sample book written in Markdown. You can use anything that Pandoc’s Markdown supports, e.g., a math equation \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\). The bookdown package can be installed from CRAN or Github: install.packages("bookdown") # or the development version # devtools::install_github("rstudio/bookdown") Remember each Rmd file contains one and only one chapter, and a chapter is defined by the first-level heading #. To compile this example to PDF, you need XeLaTeX. You are recommended to install TinyTeX (which includes XeLaTeX): https://yihui.org/tinytex/.

Chapter 2 Introduction Our lectures are going to teach you basic concepts of probability and their applications in computational finance. Our approach is based on intuition and on understanding concepts by building them with your own hands. But how can we build abstract concepts such as probability, random phenomena and chance? While probability is a mathematical theory, it gains practical value and an intuitive meaning in connection with real or conceptual experiments such as, tossing a coin, rolling two dice, playing roulette, observing the change in the index value of stocks, bonds or in exchange rates, selecting a random sample of financial transactions in a payment system and observe the number of transactions with a value below a certain amount. Many of these experiments we can nowadays do not only physically by actually rolling a pair of dice, for example but we can use the computer to build a virtual dice and roll it on the computer. We can build and simulate random phenomena. We can approach our understanding of abstract concepts by building them with our own hands on the computer. This is the approach to teaching you probability in this course. There are many ways to build virtual objects and to run simulations to manipulate them. For this we will need a programming language. The language we choose for this course is R and the integrated development environment RStudio. This is one of the main languages used in data analysis, statistics, data science and is widely used in industry and academia. This introductory chapter will introduce you to this tool in a straightforward and minimalist way to get us started. As the course develops we will gradually introduce more and more concepts of R in parallel to our main thread, the introduction to probability. We will place this introduction to R and RStudio in the context of a little project, which we then can recycle throughout the course. We will introduce R by learning how to simulate the rolling a pair of dice, a very concrete example of a process that can be described and modeled with concepts from probability and which plays a prominent role in the teaching of probability. You will learn how to: This brief introduction to R and RStudio follows very closely the book (grolemund?).
